codeReview.summary: The Copilot device code flow implementation is well-structured, thoroughly tested (16 new tests + updated existing Copilot tests + cross-area tests), and correctly replaces the old token discovery mechanism with the GitHub Device Code Flow. The code follows Go conventions, uses proper error wrapping, thread-safe data structures, and has good separation of concerns between DeviceCodeHandler and CopilotBackend.

issues.description: pollForAccessToken always starts with the hardcoded defaultPollInterval (5s) and ignores the Interval value returned in the DeviceCodeResponse. Per the OAuth Device Code Flow spec (RFC 8628 §3.3), the client SHOULD use the interval returned by the device authorization endpoint. While GitHub always returns 5s, this is technically non-compliant. The WaitForDeviceAuthorization method passes only the deviceCode string to pollForAccessToken, discarding dcResp.Interval.

Expired or abandoned device code flows are never cleaned up from the h.pending map. The only removal happens in WaitForDeviceAuthorization on success (line ~183). If the user never completes authorization, the entry stays until the process restarts. This is a minor memory leak — each entry is small — but for long-running processes it could accumulate.

The JavaScript polling logic fetches /ui/oauth/status and parses the HTML response using DOM queries (querySelectorAll, textContent matching) to determine if authorization succeeded. This is fragile — any change to the oauth_status.html template (CSS class names, text content, structure) would silently break the polling without any error indication. A dedicated JSON status endpoint (e.g., /ui/oauth/status.json?backend=copilot) would be more robust.

forceExpireToken saves a TokenData with no AccessToken field set (only GitHubToken, ExpiresAt, ObtainedAt, Source). This creates an intermediate state on disk where the persisted token file has no access_token. While the re-exchange flow in GetCopilotToken handles this correctly (it checks store.Get() and re-exchanges), the persisted state is slightly misleading — a crash at this point would leave a token file with no access token. Minor concern since the re-exchange flow recovers gracefully.

InitiateLogin uses context.Background() for both the InitiateDeviceCode call and the background goroutine that calls WaitForDeviceAuthorization. If the HTTP request is cancelled (e.g., client disconnects), the device code initiation still proceeds. This is actually desirable behavior (the flow should complete even if the user refreshes), but it means the background goroutine has no cancellation mechanism other than the device code expiry timeout from GitHub. This is documented and acceptable.

The hardcoded client_id 'Iv1.b507a08c87ecfe98' (GitHub Copilot VS Code extension) is well-documented and configurable via WithDeviceCodeClientID. Good design choice to make it overridable for testing.

sharedStateObservations.observation: The library file github-token-discovery.md documents the old token discovery approach that has now been replaced by device code flow. This file is stale and should be updated or removed to avoid confusion for future workers.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: .factory/library/github-token-discovery.md still describes env vars/gh CLI/hosts.yml discovery which is no longer used by CopilotBackend

sharedStateObservations.observation: The copilot-backend.md library file was updated by this worker and accurately reflects the new device code flow implementation, including the GitHubToken field for re-validation. This is good — the worker maintained the shared state.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: .factory/library/copilot-backend.md was updated in the same commit (800db77)

sharedStateObservations.observation: The old token_discovery.go file still exists in internal/oauth/ but is no longer imported by any production code path. The handoff notes this as a non-blocking issue for future cleanup. AGENTS.md doesn't mention this dead code, which could confuse future workers.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: internal/oauth/token_discovery.go and token_discovery_test.go still exist; copilot.go no longer references Discoverer

sharedStateObservations.observation: The worker followed the go-backend-worker skill procedure and reported one justified deviation: skipping manual curl verification because the device code flow requires real GitHub authentication that can't be simulated. The skillFeedback.followedProcedure is true.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: Handoff skillFeedback: deviation at step 7 (manual verification) — justified since device code flow needs real GitHub auth

summary: PASS. The Copilot device code flow implementation is solid and well-tested. It correctly replaces the old token discovery mechanism with the GitHub OAuth Device Code Flow matching the CLIProxyAPIPlus pattern. The code has good error handling (covers expired tokens, access denied, slow_down, context cancellation), proper thread safety (sync.RWMutex on pending flows, TokenStore is thread-safe), and comprehensive test coverage (16 device code tests, 27 Copilot backend tests, all cross-area tests pass). Issues found are all non-blocking: polling interval not using the server-specified value, minor memory leak in pending flows map, fragile HTML parsing in the JS polling, and a small gap in forceExpireToken's persisted state. None of these are correctness bugs that would block the feature.
